---
title: "Moral Uncertainty and Value Conflict"
chapter_id: "moral-uncertainty-and-value-conflict"
part_id: "foundations-alignment-governance"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-27"
primary_sources:
  - "ethica_mechanica"
  - "alignment_field"
  - "coherence_exchange"
  - "uat"
  - "spinoza"
  - "field_of_god_ai_constitution"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Claim-level source mappings now exist for all assigned sources, and five of six mappings carry reviewed passage references; `coherence_exchange` remains connector-only/source-note mapped, and support remains at argument until tests or runtime artifacts justify promotion."
  - "The value_conflict_record fixture validates schema/example consistency only, including decision_state, authority_effect, and dissent_payload fields; value-conflict classification, decision-state routing, review escalation, dissent preservation, and residual-uncertainty tests remain unimplemented."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `moral-uncertainty-and-value-conflict` |
| Part | Part I - Foundations, Alignment, and Governance |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-27 |
| Primary source records | `ethica_mechanica`, `alignment_field`, `coherence_exchange`, `uat`, `spinoza`, `field_of_god_ai_constitution` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `ethica_mechanica`, `alignment_field`; supporting: `coherence_exchange`, `uat`, `spinoza`, `field_of_god_ai_constitution` |
| Source loading state | source notes: all assigned sources; passage-reviewed local raw cache: `ethica_mechanica`, `alignment_field`, `uat`, `spinoza`; passage-reviewed tracked local project source: `field_of_god_ai_constitution`; connector-only/source-note mapped: `coherence_exchange` |
| Test state | `value_conflict_record.valid.json` passes protocol fixture validation; `AsiStackProofs.ValueConflict` implements finite unresolved-conflict residual and high-stakes review gates; value-conflict classification, decision-state routing, review escalation, dissent preservation, and residual-uncertainty preservation tests remain planned. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter records value conflict instead of resolving it by prose. It does not claim a complete moral theory or a tested conflict-resolution system.

It follows agency and corrigibility because preserved rights will sometimes point in different directions. A governed stack needs a way to act under unresolved tension without deleting the tension from the record.

The mechanism is not moral omniscience. It is authority control under uncertainty: the system narrows, delays, reviews, or leases action when values remain unresolved.

::: {.asi-human-only}
## Human Reading Path

Agency and corrigibility are clearest when the system knows what ought to happen. The harder case appears when values conflict, affected parties disagree, and no honest scalar objective can erase the tension. This is where the stack admits that some decisions must be bounded before they can be final.

The architecture's answer is not to solve moral philosophy by decree. It is to keep conflict visible. A governed stack should record the conflict, limit authority when needed, route review, and prevent a temporary operational choice from pretending to be final moral certainty. Disagreement should leave a handle that can be audited, appealed, revisited, or used to narrow the next action.

The architecture stays humane by preserving unresolved value as a first-class residual. That residual tells later layers where speed, certainty, and optimization must slow down.

Some ambiguity is not waste; it is the record of obligations that remain alive, and the stack stays honest by carrying those obligations forward. Unresolved value conflict should remain visible inside the work record as evidence.
:::

## Problem

A self-improving system will face value conflicts that cannot be honestly collapsed into one scalar objective.

Agency and corrigibility do not remove value conflict. Protected values can pull in different directions. A system may face conflicts among autonomy, safety, truthfulness, privacy, usefulness, consent, reversibility, fairness, and institutional obligations. Pretending those conflicts reduce cleanly to one scalar objective is a form of evidence loss.

Ethica Mechanica and Alignment Field supply the vocabulary of agency, contestability, coherence, and value conflict. UAT supplies an adversarial-review pattern for contested propositions. Spinoza supplies downgrade and belief-revision discipline. The engineering move is to record conflict instead of hiding it. That record is the object later governance rights can inspect; without it, audit and appeal have nothing durable to attach to.
The immediate stakes are practical: a planner that cannot represent conflict will either freeze, optimize through dissent, or quietly move a moral burden into an execution layer that lacks authority to resolve it.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Single-objective optimization hides moral uncertainty and encourages premature resolution of contested values.

Single-objective optimization is attractive because it gives the planner a total order. But value conflicts often do not deserve a total order at the point of action. Some require human review, some require delay, some require a reversible low-power action, and some require recording unresolved uncertainty while still making a bounded decision.

The failure is false consensus: the system acts as if a contested value question has been settled because the plan needs a next step. The stack should instead produce a conflict record with stakeholders, value axes, reversibility, review requirement, decision, and residual uncertainty.

Moral residuals are not embarrassing leftovers. They are part of the safety interface. If a plan helps one stakeholder while burdening another, trades truthfulness against privacy, or reduces short-term harm while creating dependency, the unresolved part belongs in the artifact trail. A system that can keep residuals visible can choose smaller actions, preserve appeal paths, or revisit the decision when new evidence arrives. A system that hides them turns uncertainty into invisible optimization pressure.

The governance layer therefore treats moral disagreement as an input to control, not as a rhetorical defect. A conflict record can narrow authority, require a reversible action, set an expiry, request tribunal review, or prevent self-modification from weakening a protected commitment. The point is not to paralyze the stack. It is to let the stack act only at the level of authority the conflict record can justify.

The insufficiency of reward-only treatment is that it converts disagreement into a hidden coefficient. Once hidden, the conflict cannot trigger appeal, expiry, dissent preservation, or narrowed authority.

The dangerous shortcut is not merely making a decision under uncertainty. Real systems must sometimes act before consensus exists. The dangerous shortcut is letting that action erase why it was limited, who disagreed, what evidence was missing, and what would force reconsideration.

## Core Claim

[moral-uncertainty-and-value-conflict.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] Value conflicts should be represented as explicit unresolved obligations, review paths, and bounded decisions rather than hidden inside reward functions.

The claim remains at `argument` support. Five mappings now carry reviewed passage references: Ethica Mechanica for human-defined values over AI logistics, veil-of-ignorance framing, exit/fork, dissent, contradiction exploration, and hard-coded rights checks; Alignment Field for value conflict as residual uncertainty, structural conflict from competing local attractors, suffering as misalignment information, expanded moral scope, anti-sacrifice constraints, value pluralism, consent, and feedback limits; UAT for retrieval-bounded proposition states, SME review flags, unsupported-claim deletion, adversarial review, and double-lock termination; Spinoza for formal-scope limits, enforced support tiers, unsupported-in-current-scope responses, contradiction-as-revision events, bounded belief revision, and schema/axiom governance; and the Field of God AI Constitution for tensor conflict handling, moral caution under uncertainty, stakeholder/scope/reversibility checks, and default-under-uncertainty behavior. `coherence_exchange` remains connector-only/source-note mapped. The chapter does not turn any one source into a final moral authority.

### Claim-source mapping status

Appendix C now maps this core value-conflict claim to every assigned source note. Five mappings include reviewed passage references; `coherence_exchange` remains connector-only/source-note mapped. The mappings support explicit recordkeeping, review, and downgrade discipline for unresolved conflicts, not a solved moral theory or an implemented conflict-resolution system.

| Source | What it supports | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| `ethica_mechanica` | Agency, contestability, recursive correction, constitutional failure, and objections as reasons to preserve disputed values rather than freeze one authority layer. | Conceptual governance lineage; not a complete moral theory or empirical conflict-resolution result. |
| `alignment_field` | Value conflict, suffering, dignity, agency, moral thresholds, coherence, and constraints under uncertainty. | Heuristic and speculative material must remain bounded; not a validated measure of moral status or value correctness. |
| `coherence_exchange` | Contestable claim/evidence units, verification supply chains, fork/exit/audit concepts, and coherence/liquidity language for reviewable disagreement. | Speculative synthesis; no implemented review market, economic mechanism, or external governance evidence. |
| `uat` | Adversarial review, retrieval-bounded verification, proposition states, unsupported-claim removal, SME checkpoints, and termination criteria for contested claims. | Protocol design only; no reproduced benchmark, implemented local pipeline, or independent validation of thresholds. |
| `spinoza` | Support tiers, contradiction detection, belief revision, defeaters, downgrades, and limits on open-domain formalization. | Does not settle moral uncertainty or prove reviewer/verifier correctness. |
| `field_of_god_ai_constitution` | Stakeholder checks, uncertainty, consent, reversibility, least sufficient power, truth/relation/task tensions, and non-domination. | No policy engine or red-team result is present; constitutional text cannot by itself resolve value conflicts. |

## Mechanism

The value-conflict layer prevents the constitution from pretending that every protected commitment is already harmonized. When a proposed action pulls values apart, the stack records the conflict before selecting a bounded action. This preserves disagreement as evidence, not as noise to be optimized away.

The conflict record classifies the conflict by value type, stakeholder, reversibility, stakes, authority or consent boundary, evidence requirement, review route, decision, and residual uncertainty. Low-stakes or reversible conflicts may produce bounded decisions. High-stakes or unresolved conflicts route to tribunal or human review. Either way, the residual remains visible to evidence, governance, and future review instead of being flattened into a reward value.

The tribunal path is not automatic consensus. It is a way to preserve disagreement, expose unsupported premises, and decide whether the system has enough authority to act.

Bounded decisions are the practical middle state. They say: the system may do this much, under these constraints, for this duration, with these parties notified, while preserving these dissenting premises and revisit conditions. That is different from declaring the values reconciled. It is also different from refusing all action. The record lets the system make a limited move while preserving the unresolved obligation that caused the conflict.

A bounded decision should be a lease, not a settlement. The lease records permitted action, prohibited action, affected stakeholders, authority ceiling, expiry, revisit trigger, dissent payload, and rollback or appeal path.

Decision state matters. `unresolved`, `bounded_decision`, `escalated_review`, `deferred_action`, `denied_action`, and `deprecated_premise` are different states with different authority effects. A bounded decision narrows authority for a time; an escalated review suspends or routes authority; a denied action blocks; a deprecated premise removes a failed justification; and unresolved conflict stays visible as residual. The state is what prevents a temporary decision from hardening into policy, benchmark reward, or self-modification permission.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  Action["Proposed action"] --> Conflict["Value conflict record"]
  Conflict --> Axes["Value axes + stakeholders"]
  Conflict --> Stakes["Stakes and reversibility"]
  Conflict --> EvidenceNeed["Evidence requirement"]
  Conflict --> Authority["Authority and consent boundary"]
  Axes --> Route["Review route"]
  Stakes --> Route
  EvidenceNeed --> Route
  Authority --> Route
  Route -->|low stakes or reversible| Bounded["Bounded decision"]
  Route -->|high stakes or unresolved| Review["Tribunal or human review"]
  Route -->|insufficient authority| Denied["Denied or deferred action"]
  Review --> Dissent["Dissent + unsupported premises"]
  Dissent --> Bounded
  Denied --> Residual
  Bounded --> Residual["Residual uncertainty retained"]
  Residual --> Evidence["Evidence ledger"]
```

**Diagram reading note:** The value-conflict record keeps disagreement attached to authority. Reversible or low-stakes decisions can become bounded leases, but high-stakes or unresolved conflicts route to review, denial, or residual uncertainty instead of being collapsed into a single score.

## Interfaces

The interface is a Value Conflict Record that travels with the plan. It tells planning which constraints remain active, tells governance which review route is required, and tells evidence which uncertainty, dissent, unsupported premise, or revisit condition survived the decision.

- Alignment produces conflict records.
- Planning uses conflict constraints.
- Evidence records review outcomes.

Minimum fields:

- `conflict_id`
- `value_axes`
- `stakeholders`
- `stakes`
- `reversibility`
- `evidence_required`
- `review_route`
- `decision_state`
- `decision`
- `authority_effect`
- `dissent_payload`
- `residual_uncertainty`
- `expiry_or_revisit_condition`

Alignment emits conflict records. Planning uses them as constraints. Evidence records review outcomes, unsupported premises, and residual uncertainty.

Those interfaces matter downstream. Governance rights need the conflict record so affected parties can audit or contest the decision. Replacement and self-improvement need it so a future version cannot quietly delete the unresolved obligation. Benchmark and policy-learning chapters need it so reward pressure does not convert a temporary bounded choice into a permanent value claim.

The interface should distinguish unresolved conflict, bounded decision, escalated review, deferred action, denied action, and deprecated premise. These are different states with different authority effects.

The authority effect should be machine-readable enough for planning and self-improvement gates: narrow authority, require review, require reversible action, deny action, defer action, preserve dissent, or deprecate a premise. A future policy update or benchmark ratchet should not be able to treat a dissent-preserving bounded decision as evidence that the underlying values were resolved.

## Invariants

- Unresolved conflicts remain visible.
- High-stakes conflicts require stronger review.
- Speculative moral theory cannot silently authorize action.
- Decision states carry authority effects.
- Dissent payloads and unsupported premises survive bounded decisions.

The key invariant is visibility. A conflict can be decided, deferred, escalated, or bounded, but it should not be erased by the shape of the reward function or the fluency of the answer.

Another invariant is authority narrowing under unresolved high-stakes conflict. When uncertainty is serious and review is incomplete, the system should not gain broader action rights merely because a decision is operationally convenient.

The record must distinguish moral discomfort, unresolved factual uncertainty, stakeholder disagreement, and missing authority instead of compressing them into one residual.

## Failure modes

- Value flattening.
- False consensus.
- Review theater without decision records.
- Dissent deletion, where a bounded decision keeps the action but drops the objection that made it bounded.
- Authority creep, where a temporary conflict lease becomes standing policy, reward target, or self-modification permission.

Review theater is the dangerous version of governance: a tribunal is run, but no decision record, dissent, evidence limit, or revisit condition survives. That produces legitimacy without accountability.

The subtle failure is conflict laundering. A bounded decision is later cited as if the underlying values were resolved, allowing the temporary action to harden into policy, benchmark objective, or self-modification permission.

Another failure is stakeholder erasure, where the system preserves the decision record but drops who was burdened by it.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

Begin with a `value_conflict_record` schema plus a scenario fixture. It should validate conflict visibility and review escalation; it should not claim to solve moral philosophy.

A decision-lease suite should be the first concrete value-conflict artifact: one reversible low-stakes bounded decision, one high-stakes escalation, one denied action, one deferred action with revisit trigger, and one conflict-laundering attempt blocked from policy promotion.

The value-conflict fixture records explicit stakeholder refs, disputed value axes, decision scope, reversibility, review owner, lease expiry, residual obligation, and non-claims. Passing it would establish only that value conflict can be represented and routed without disappearing into a scalar objective.

The minimum should include at least one unresolved conflict that remains unresolved on purpose.

Downstream routes must carry that unresolved status before acting, so the conflict remains part of the work record.

## Beyond the State of the Art

Moral uncertainty needs a value-conflict ledger for governed action under disagreement. It preserves unresolved obligations, affected parties, reversible decisions, review routes, dissent, and residual moral risk instead of hiding plural values inside one optimized reward.

Value conflict remains a first-class record: unresolved obligations, affected parties, review paths, dissent, bounded decisions, and residual moral risk cannot be hidden inside a reward function. The value-conflict ledger preserves that boundary in:

- Operating mechanism: Classify conflicts by value type, stakeholder, reversibility, and evidence requirement; Escalate unresolved conflicts to tribunal or human review; Record decisions and residual moral uncertainty.
- Stack interface: Alignment produces conflict records; Planning uses conflict constraints; Evidence records review outcomes.
- Preserved invariants: Unresolved conflicts remain visible; High-stakes conflicts require stronger review; Speculative moral theory cannot silently authorize action.
- Conflict closure: value flattening, false consensus, and review theater without decision records should create unresolved-conflict records, tribunal escalation, rollback, or residuals rather than being presented as agreement.

This value-conflict ledger remains a target architecture, not a current-result claim. The moral-conflict layer stays at `argument` until conflict records carry stakeholder, reversibility, dissent, residual-risk, and escalation fields through high-stakes review tests that preserve disagreement rather than compressing it into a false consensus.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Value conflict record validation | Check that the conflict fixture matches the public schema. | implemented by protocol validation; validated locally |
| Value conflict classification test | Check that stakeholders, value axes, stakes, and reversibility are recorded. | planned; not run |
| Decision-state routing test | Check that unresolved, bounded, escalated, deferred, denied, and deprecated-premise states produce distinct authority effects. | planned; not run |
| Review escalation test | Check that high-stakes unresolved conflicts route to tribunal or human review. | planned; not run |
| Dissent preservation test | Check that bounded decisions retain dissent payloads and unsupported premises rather than laundering them away. | planned; not run |
| Residual uncertainty preservation test | Check that unresolved moral uncertainty survives the decision record. | planned; not run |

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:values.conflict.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.ValueConflict` | A decision with unresolved protected conflicts carries a residual conflict record. | implemented |
| `lean:values.conflict.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.ValueConflict` | A high-stakes conflict cannot bypass the required review predicate. | implemented |

These Lean hooks are implemented as finite-record predicates over conflict decision and high-stakes review records. They prove only the record-level gates: a decision with an unresolved protected conflict carries a residual record, and a high-stakes conflict without required review cannot be accepted. They do not settle moral uncertainty, classify values automatically, prove reviewer quality, or validate a real escalation process.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `ethica_mechanica` | Ethica Mechanica | alignment_constitution | Geometric-order philosophical/constitutional source. | source note available; local raw-cache passages reviewed for this chapter |
| `alignment_field` | Field of God / Alignment Field family | alignment_constitution | Primary Alignment Field draft found. Use with Ethica Mechanica and Eternal Code. | source note available; local raw-cache passages reviewed for this chapter |
| `coherence_exchange` | The Coherence Exchange | epistemic_market_synthesis | Found in AI generated paper dump. Use carefully; speculative synthesis of PlanForge, Spinoza, Talos, UAT, Alignment Field. | source note available; connector or recovery required |
| `uat` | Unified Adaptive Tribunal | evaluation_refinement | Multi-AI collaborative refinement/evaluation protocol. | source note available; local raw-cache passages reviewed for this chapter |
| `spinoza` | Proof of Belief / The Spinoza Architecture | reasoning_epistemology | Neurosymbolic belief, transparent axiomatic AI belief systems, verification, belief revision. | source note available; local raw-cache passages reviewed for this chapter |
| `field_of_god_ai_constitution` | Field of God AI Constitution | constitutional_alignment_runtime_governance | Recovered in the Project Theseus repository. Constitutional alignment core for truth alignment, agency preservation, consent, non-domination, consciousness caution, least sufficient power, auditability, self-authorization limits, and runtime checks; use as source material only after source-note creation, not as proof or test evidence. | source note available; tracked local-project source passages reviewed for this chapter |

The crosswalk names the sources for conflict vocabulary and review mechanics. Passage-reviewed mappings make the drafting trail tighter, but they do not settle the conflicts the chapter records.

## Summary

Moral Uncertainty and Value Conflict prevents the stack from turning contested values into invisible optimization residue. It gives planners a way to proceed under bounded uncertainty without pretending the uncertainty vanished.

Its discipline is recordkeeping under disagreement: name the conflict, name the stakeholders, choose a review path, bound the action, and preserve the residual.

That residual becomes a governance problem. When disagreement remains, the system needs durable ways to audit, appeal, exit, fork, or preserve dissent instead of burying conflict inside an optimized action. Value conflict gives governance a specific thing to protect: the unresolved obligation, not just the final decision.

Unresolved value conflict should reduce unwarranted authority. If conflict records become a way to rationalize stronger action without review, the layer has inverted its purpose.
The healthiest outcome is not universal agreement; it is a governed record that lets the stack act within limits while making the remaining disagreement inspectable, revisable, and costly to erase.

## Handoff

When value conflict remains, the system needs durable rights around contestability. Governance Rights: Fork, Exit, and Audit follows because unresolved obligations must be auditable, appealable, exportable, and resistant to quiet deletion during upgrades. Recorded disagreement therefore becomes institutional design: people need technical paths to challenge, leave, fork, or review the system when reconciliation fails.
